long_form_blocks.heading: What is SuiteCRM?
paragraphs: SuiteCRM is an open-source customer relationship management platform built to give organizations a 360-degree view of customers and business operations. It covers sales, marketing and customer service through a flexible data model, reporting dashboards, lead and opportunity management, and automated segmentation.

The software itself is free to download and self-host, with no per-user licensing fees. SuiteCRM Ltd, which develops the platform, also sells optional managed hosting, implementation and support packages, including a warranty-backed SuiteASSURED plan, for teams that want additional assurance or don't have in-house IT.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who SuiteCRM is for
paragraphs: SuiteCRM suits organizations looking for a customizable, lower-cost alternative to proprietary CRM platforms, from technical teams that want to self-host and extend the open REST API, to businesses that prefer fully managed hosting and professional support instead of running their own servers.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is EspoCRM?
paragraphs: EspoCRM is a web-based CRM application for tracking contacts, companies, projects and opportunities. It is open source and can be self-hosted for free on Windows, macOS or Linux, including via Docker.

EspoCRM also offers cloud-hosted plans on Google Cloud, AWS or Azure, priced per user per month across Basic, Enterprise and Ultimate tiers. All cloud plans include API access, over 2,000 integrations and a set of official extensions at no extra cost.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Monica?
paragraphs: Monica is an open-source personal CRM for organizing information about the people in your life, from family and friends to broader personal connections. It supports contact management, activity logging, reminders, and a personal journal.

Monica offers a free hosted plan limited to 10 contacts, a paid hosted plan at $9/month (or $90/year) with unlimited contacts and no per-contact fees, and a self-hosted option using the open-source code, which is free to run on your own server.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Monica is for
paragraphs: Monica is built for individuals who want to keep track of relationships and personal details without advertising or data monetization, whether they choose the affordable hosted plan or self-host for full control.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Dolibarr?
paragraphs: Dolibarr is a free, open-source ERP and CRM suite that brings together customer management, HR, finance, inventory and project tools in one web application. It is overseen by the Dolibarr Association, founded in 2009.

The software is free to download and self-host on Windows, Mac, Linux, Docker or the cloud. Paid hosting and support are available, but only through third-party partner companies rather than Dolibarr itself.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Dolibarr is for
paragraphs: Dolibarr is designed for SMEs, large companies, freelancers and associations that want an integrated, free ERP and CRM system they can run themselves or deploy with a hosting partner.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is ERPNext?
paragraphs: ERPNext is a free and open-source ERP developed by Frappe Technologies, covering accounting, procurement, sales, CRM, inventory, manufacturing, projects, point of sale, HR/payroll, quality management and helpdesk functions in a single platform.

ERPNext can be self-hosted at no cost, or run on Frappe Cloud, the company's managed hosting service, with compute-based pricing starting at $14/month and scaling to $108/month depending on usage. Frappe Technologies also supports a network of 200+ certified implementation partners.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who ERPNext is for

long_form_blocks.heading: What is metasfresh?
paragraphs: metasfresh is a cloud-based ERP built on open-source technology and operated by metas GmbH, focused on mid-sized companies in the DACH region (Germany, Austria, Switzerland). It covers the full supply chain: planning and demand forecasting, procurement and goods receipt, production and warehouse logistics, shipping and distribution, and billing with traceability.

The system has been Fraunhofer IML-validated since March 2022. Pricing is a hybrid model: a custom-quoted Starter implementation package, plus ongoing user fees of €79-99/month and cloud hosting from €249-299/month, hosted in German data centers, all excluding VAT.

features.title: Dynamic org charts
features.description: Visualizes reporting structures, departments, and cross-functional teams that update automatically.

features.title: Employee directory
features.description: Detailed view of employee roles, departments, and team composition.

features.title: Custom profiles
features.description: Displays colleague interests and skills to support collaboration.

features.title: Who's Who game
features.description: An interactive face-to-name matching activity for team engagement.

features.title: Planning org charts
features.description: Private charts for scenario planning around resource allocation, with commenting and feedback.

features.title: HRIS data sync
features.description: Imports employee data via automated HRIS integrations or Excel upload.

pros: Founded in 2013 with an established org chart product
Now backed by Workleap's broader employee experience ecosystem
Used by over 70,000 leaders worldwide across Workleap's product suite
Supports both automated HRIS sync and manual Excel import
cons: Acquired by Workleap; pricing and product direction now reflect Workleap's broader suite rather than a standalone company
Historical published pricing (from $149/month for 20 users) may not reflect current Workleap pricing
No AI capabilities are mentioned for the product

faqs.question: When was Pingboard founded?
faqs.answer: Pingboard was founded in 2013 by Rob Eanes and Bill Boebel.

faqs.question: Is Pingboard still an independent company?
faqs.answer: No, Pingboard is now part of Workleap and operates as one of its employee experience products, still under the Pingboard name.

faqs.question: What does Pingboard's org chart tool do?
faqs.answer: It creates dynamic, automatically updating org charts showing reporting lines, departments, and cross-functional teams, built from HRIS data or an Excel upload.

faqs.question: Does Pingboard have an employee directory?
faqs.answer: Yes, Pingboard includes an employee directory with custom profiles showing roles, departments, and team composition.

use_cases.title: Visualizing company structure
use_cases.description: HR teams give remote and hybrid employees visibility into reporting lines and team composition.

use_cases.title: Workforce scenario planning
use_cases.description: Leaders use private planning org charts to model resource allocation changes before rolling them out.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Pingboard?
paragraphs: Pingboard is a cloud-based org chart and employee directory tool founded in 2013 and headquartered in Austin, Texas. It gives distributed and hybrid organizations dynamic visibility into reporting structures and team composition.
Pingboard has been acquired and now operates as part of Workleap's employee experience product suite, still under the Pingboard name, alongside Workleap's other engagement and recognition tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Pingboard is for
paragraphs: Pingboard is used by HR and People Ops teams managing remote or hybrid workforces who need an up-to-date org chart and employee directory synced from their HRIS.

enrichment.short_description: Organizational visibility platform for automated org charts, employee directories, and people search, based in Detroit.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Sift Org Chart provides automated org charts, employee directories, and people search with a 14-day free trial, from $150/month.

features.title: Automated org charts
features.description: Builds org charts from existing people data with real-time updates, including dotted-line relationships and open roles.

features.title: Employee profiles
features.description: Customizable profiles showing skills, interests, pronouns, languages, and work experience.

features.title: Search and directory
features.description: Lets employees search, filter, and find colleagues by role, skill, or work experience.

features.title: Pages
features.description: Content organization capability within the platform.

features.title: Analytics
features.description: 13 prebuilt organizational analytics reports on the Directory plan.

features.title: API access
features.description: Developer API access available on the Directory and higher plans.

pros: 14-day free trial with full platform access
Serves 500,000+ employees across client organizations
Integrates with Microsoft Teams and HRIS/identity systems
Rated 4.8/5 on G2 and 4.6/5 on Capterra
Volume, long-term, and 15% nonprofit discounts available
cons: Minimum of 20 users required on the base plan
Additional per-user fees apply on top of the base monthly price
Enterprise plan requires custom pricing for organizations with 1,000+ employees
Founding year and company size are not disclosed

faqs.question: Does Sift offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, Sift offers a 14-day free trial with full platform access.

faqs.question: How much does Sift cost?
faqs.answer: Sift Org Chart starts at $150/month paid annually, Directory starts at $175/month, and Sift Complete starts at $200/month, each with additional per-user fees and a 20-user minimum.

faqs.question: Where is Sift based?
faqs.answer: Sift is headquartered in Detroit, Michigan.

faqs.question: Does Sift have mobile apps?
faqs.answer: Yes, Sift offers iOS and Android apps.

use_cases.title: Automated org chart maintenance
use_cases.description: HR teams keep org charts current automatically instead of manually updating slides or spreadsheets.

use_cases.title: Finding the right colleague
use_cases.description: Employees search the directory by skill or role to find the right person for a project.
use_cases.audience: Employees at mid-size and enterprise organizations

features: Combines Org Chart and Directory capabilities

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Sift Org Chart?
paragraphs: Sift Org Chart is an organizational visibility platform, headquartered in Detroit, that automatically builds org charts and employee directories from a company's existing people data.
Plans start at $150/month for Org Chart, $175/month for Directory, and $200/month for the combined Sift Complete, each billed annually with a 20-user minimum and a 14-day free trial.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Sift Org Chart is for
paragraphs: Sift serves mid-sized and enterprise organizations, including companies like Sumitomo, Rocket Companies, Grant Thornton, and Ping Identity, that want employees to easily find colleagues and understand company structure.

enrichment.short_description: Knowledge base and documentation software with a built-in AI chatbot, plans starting at $100/month.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: KnowledgeOwl is knowledge base software with a closed-system AI chatbot, white-glove migration, and plans from $100/month.

features.title: Unlimited nested categories
features.description: Organize articles with drag-and-drop category reorganization.

features.title: Built-in AI chatbot
features.description: A closed-system chatbot that only answers using content from your knowledge base and shows its sources.

features.title: White-glove migration
features.description: Assisted migration from platforms like Confluence, Zendesk, and SharePoint.

features.title: Custom theme design
features.description: Branding and theme customization for the knowledge base.

features.title: Analytics and deflection tracking
features.description: Built-in analytics with reader reports, ranging from 45 to 450+ days of data depending on plan.

features.title: Role-based access controls
features.description: Controls who can view or edit specific content.

features.title: Version history
features.description: Tracks document revisions over time.

pros: Closed-system AI chatbot only answers from your own content and cites its sources
White-glove migration and theme build included on every paid plan
1:1 onboarding calls included, more frequent on higher tiers
Supports GDPR and HIPAA compliance needs
In business for 11+ years
cons: No free tier; entry plan starts at $100/month
Article limits apply per plan (1,000 on Basic, 2,500 on Pro, 5,000 on Business)
AI credits are capped by plan (500 on Pro, 1,000 on Business) rather than unlimited
Additional authors ($25/month) and knowledge bases ($50/month) cost extra
Headquarters and founding year are not disclosed on the site

faqs.question: Does KnowledgeOwl have a free plan?
faqs.answer: No, KnowledgeOwl's plans start at $100/month for the Basic tier; there is no free tier.

faqs.question: How does KnowledgeOwl's AI chatbot work?
faqs.answer: It is a closed-system AI chatbot that only answers using content from your own knowledge base, always shows its sources, and does not use your data to train external LLMs.

faqs.question: What platforms can KnowledgeOwl migrate from?
faqs.answer: KnowledgeOwl offers white-glove migration from platforms including Confluence, Zendesk, and SharePoint.

faqs.question: How long has KnowledgeOwl been around?
faqs.answer: KnowledgeOwl states it has been in business for 11+ years.

use_cases.title: Replacing scattered internal docs
use_cases.description: Teams consolidate internal and external documentation that was previously spread across Confluence, SharePoint, or shared drives.
use_cases.audience: IT, support, and knowledge management teams

use_cases.title: Customer self-service with AI answers
use_cases.description: Support teams deploy the built-in AI chatbot so customers get sourced answers directly from published documentation.

features: AI chatbot, search, and article creation
90 days of data plus reader reports
450 days of data plus reader reports

long_form_blocks.heading: What is KnowledgeOwl?
paragraphs: KnowledgeOwl is knowledge base and documentation software that lets teams create and share internal and external documentation from one platform. It includes a closed-system AI chatbot that only answers using content from the customer's own knowledge base and cites its sources.

Paid plans start at $100/month for the Basic tier and scale up to Business at $500/month, with an Enterprise tier offering unlimited articles, AI credits, and analytics. Every paid plan includes white-glove migration and a custom theme build.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who KnowledgeOwl is for
paragraphs: KnowledgeOwl targets organizations across healthcare, energy, financial services, education, manufacturing, IT services, and call centers that want to move off Confluence, SharePoint, or scattered documents into a dedicated knowledge base.

enrichment.short_description: GitBook is a Git-synced documentation platform with an AI assistant, API playgrounds, and enterprise-grade security.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: GitBook is a Git-synced documentation platform with an AI assistant and API playgrounds. Free plan available; paid plans start at $65/site/month.

features.title: Git Sync
features.description: Sync documentation content with GitHub or GitLab repositories using branch-based workflows.

features.title: GitBook Agent
features.description: An AI agent that detects outdated content in your documentation and flags it for review.

features.title: AI Assistant
features.description: Lets readers ask questions and get answers sourced directly from your documentation (Ultimate/Enterprise plans).

features.title: Interactive API playgrounds
features.description: Lets users test API calls directly from within API reference pages.

features.title: Block-based visual editor
features.description: A block editor for building pages with custom content blocks.

features.title: Enterprise security
features.description: SOC 2 and ISO 27001 certified infrastructure, with SAML SSO and access controls on higher tiers.

features.title: Authenticated content
features.description: Restrict or personalize documentation access for signed-in readers on Ultimate and Enterprise plans.

pros: Free plan available with no credit card required
Git-based sync fits developer workflows (GitHub/GitLab)
Built-in AI assistant and content-freshness agent
SOC 2 and ISO 27001 certified security
Interactive API playgrounds built into docs
cons: Premium and Ultimate plans are priced per site plus a per-user add-on charge
AI Assistant and GitBook Agent usage are capped on the Free and Premium tiers
Enterprise features like SAML SSO require custom/quote-only pricing

faqs.question: Does GitBook have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, GitBook offers a free plan with no credit card required, including the block editor, Git sync, and a GitBook Agent limited to 10 messages per week.

faqs.question: How much does GitBook cost?
faqs.answer: Paid plans start at $65 per site/month (billed annually) for Premium, with Ultimate at $249 per site/month; both add $12/month per user beyond the first.

faqs.question: Is there a free trial for paid plans?
faqs.answer: Yes, Premium and Ultimate plans include a 14-day free trial.

faqs.question: Does GitBook sync with GitHub or GitLab?
faqs.answer: Yes, GitBook's Git Sync feature supports branch-based workflows with GitHub and GitLab.

faqs.question: What security certifications does GitBook have?
faqs.answer: GitBook's Free plan is ISO 27001 and SOC 2 certified; higher tiers add SAML SSO and Git Sync IP allowlisting.

use_cases.title: Developer documentation
use_cases.description: Teams sync docs to a GitHub or GitLab repo and publish API references with interactive playgrounds.

use_cases.title: Internal knowledge bases
use_cases.description: Ultimate and Enterprise plans support authenticated, permissioned content for internal-only docs.
use_cases.audience: Enterprise IT and internal ops teams

use_cases.title: Customer-facing product docs
use_cases.description: Teams publish branded public documentation sites with AI-powered search and an assistant that answers reader questions.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is GitBook?
paragraphs: GitBook is a documentation platform that connects content to a Git repository, letting teams write, review, and publish docs using branch-based workflows.

The platform layers AI features on top of docs, including an assistant that answers reader questions and an agent that flags outdated pages, plus a free plan supporting GitHub/GitLab sync and a block-based editor.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who GitBook is for
paragraphs: GitBook suits engineering and DevRel teams that already work in Git and want documentation to follow the same branch/PR review process as their code.
Its higher tiers add SSO, authenticated content, and compliance certifications (SOC 2, ISO 27001) aimed at larger companies publishing both internal and customer-facing docs.

enrichment.short_description: BookStack is a free, open-source, self-hosted wiki platform for organizing documentation into books, chapters, and pages.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: BookStack: a free, MIT-licensed, self-hosted wiki app for organizing docs into books, chapters, and pages, with SSO, MFA, and a built-in diagram editor.

features.title: Books, Chapters & Pages structure
features.description: Organizes content hierarchically into Books, Chapters, and Pages for easy navigation.

features.title: WYSIWYG and Markdown editors
features.description: Offers a visual editor as well as an optional Markdown editor with live preview.

features.title: Built-in diagrams
features.description: Includes diagrams.net drawing tools directly inside the page editor.

features.title: Full-text search
features.description: Search across all content, with the ability to link directly to specific paragraphs.

features.title: Enterprise authentication
features.description: Supports OIDC, SAML2, and LDAP for self-hosted and enterprise login.

features.title: Multi-factor authentication
features.description: Supports TOTP-based MFA with backup codes.

features.title: Roles & permissions
features.description: A role and permission system controls access to content.

features.title: Dark/light themes
features.description: Includes built-in dark and light UI themes.

pros: Fully free and open source (MIT licensed)
Lightweight enough to run on low-cost VPS hosting
Built-in diagram editor (diagrams.net) with no extra setup
Enterprise-ready auth via OIDC, SAML2, and LDAP
Simple Books > Chapters > Pages structure
cons: Self-hosted only — no official managed cloud/hosting plan
Requires a PHP/Laravel/MySQL stack to install and maintain
No official native mobile apps

faqs.question: Is BookStack free?
faqs.answer: Yes, BookStack is fully free and open source under the MIT license.

faqs.question: Does BookStack require a database?
faqs.answer: Yes, it runs on PHP with the Laravel framework and uses MySQL for storage.

faqs.question: Can BookStack run on cheap hosting?
faqs.answer: Yes — according to the project, it can run happily on a budget VPS such as a €2.50/month IONOS VPS.

faqs.question: Does BookStack support single sign-on?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports OIDC, SAML2, and LDAP authentication for self-hosted and enterprise environments.

faqs.question: Where is the BookStack source code hosted?
faqs.answer: The source code is available on Codeberg.

use_cases.title: Team documentation
use_cases.description: Organizations use BookStack to host internal documentation organized into books and chapters.
use_cases.audience: IT teams and internal knowledge managers

use_cases.title: Confluence alternative
use_cases.description: Self-hosted teams use BookStack as a lower-cost alternative to enterprise wiki tools.

use_cases.title: Personal or project wikis
use_cases.description: Individuals and open-source projects self-host BookStack for organizing notes and project documentation.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is BookStack?
paragraphs: BookStack is a self-hosted, open-source platform for organizing and storing documentation, using a Books, Chapters, and Pages hierarchy that keeps large amounts of content navigable.
It's released under the MIT license, runs on PHP/Laravel with a MySQL database, and includes a WYSIWYG editor, a Markdown editor, and a built-in diagrams.net drawing tool.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who BookStack is for
paragraphs: BookStack fits teams and individuals who want to self-host their documentation rather than pay for a hosted SaaS wiki, and who are comfortable running a PHP/MySQL application on their own server.
Its support for OIDC, SAML2, and LDAP also makes it usable in enterprise environments that need centralized authentication.

enrichment.short_description: Wiki.js is a free, open-source wiki platform with multiple editors, full version history, and support for over 40 languages.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Wiki.js: a free, open-source (AGPL-3.0) wiki app with Markdown/WYSIWYG editors, full version history, and support for 40+ languages including RTL.

features.title: Multiple editors
features.description: Choose from Markdown, visual WYSIWYG, plain HTML, and other editors for each page.

features.title: Full version history
features.description: All content changes are tracked, so pages can be reverted or deleted pages recovered at any time.

features.title: 40+ languages with RTL support
features.description: The interface is available in over 40 languages, including native right-to-left language support.

features.title: Flexible search
features.description: Includes a built-in database search plus integrations with Algolia and Elasticsearch.

features.title: Granular permissions
features.description: Supports user groups with page-level access controls.

features.title: Multiple storage backends
features.description: Content can sync to Git, or use cloud storage like AWS S3 and Azure, alongside local/network backups.

features.title: Container-friendly deployment
features.description: Runs on Docker, Kubernetes, Linux, macOS, and Windows Server, with support for PostgreSQL, MySQL, MariaDB, MS SQL Server, and SQLite.

pros: Free and open source (AGPL-3.0 license)
Full page version history with revert/recovery
Wide language support including RTL languages
Multiple database backends supported
One-click DigitalOcean Marketplace install for easy hosting
cons: Self-hosted — no official managed cloud plan
AGPL-3.0 licensing may affect commercial derivative products
Advanced search (Algolia/Elasticsearch) requires external services

faqs.question: Is Wiki.js free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Wiki.js is free and open source, released under the AGPL-3.0 license.

faqs.question: How many languages does Wiki.js support?
faqs.answer: The interface is available in over 40 languages, including right-to-left languages.

faqs.question: What databases does Wiki.js support?
faqs.answer: PostgreSQL, MySQL, MariaDB, MS SQL Server, and SQLite.

faqs.question: Can I deploy Wiki.js with Docker?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports Docker and Kubernetes deployments, as well as a one-click installer on the DigitalOcean Marketplace.

faqs.question: How popular is Wiki.js?
faqs.answer: It has over 100 million downloads and more than 28,100 stars on GitHub.

use_cases.title: Engineering team documentation
use_cases.description: Teams use Wiki.js's Git sync and version history to manage technical documentation alongside code.

use_cases.title: Multilingual knowledge bases
use_cases.description: Organizations serving global or RTL-language audiences use Wiki.js's 40+ language interface.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted internal wikis
use_cases.description: Teams that need full control over their data self-host Wiki.js via Docker or Kubernetes.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Wiki.js?
paragraphs: Wiki.js is a free, open-source wiki engine released under the AGPL-3.0 license, offering multiple content editors (Markdown, WYSIWYG, and plain HTML) and full version history for every page.

It supports several database backends (PostgreSQL, MySQL, MariaDB, MS SQL Server, SQLite) and storage integrations such as Git sync and AWS S3/Azure, and can be deployed via Docker, Kubernetes, or a one-click DigitalOcean installer.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Wiki.js is for
paragraphs: Wiki.js suits teams that want a self-hosted, modular wiki they can extend with only the modules they need, rather than a one-size-fits-all tool.
Its 40+ language interface with RTL support also makes it a fit for international or multilingual documentation projects.

enrichment.short_description: DokuWiki is a free, open-source wiki engine that stores all content as plain text files, with no database required.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: DokuWiki: a free, open-source (GPLv2) wiki engine with flat-file storage, no database required, maintained since 2004 by Andreas Gohr and the community.

features.title: Flat-file storage
features.description: Stores all wiki pages and revisions as plain text files rather than in a database.

features.title: No database required
features.description: Runs without needing to set up or maintain a separate database server.

features.title: Access control lists
features.description: Supports permission rules to control who can read or edit pages.

features.title: Revision history
features.description: Tracks page changes so prior versions can be reviewed or restored.

features.title: Plugin and template ecosystem
features.description: Extensible through a large library of community plugins and templates.

features.title: Simple markup syntax
features.description: Uses a lightweight wiki markup syntax for formatting pages.

pros: Free and open source (GPL-2.0 license)
No database to install, back up, or maintain (flat-file storage)
Low system requirements, only needs PHP
Actively maintained since 2004 with a large plugin ecosystem
Over 4,700 GitHub stars and hundreds of forks
cons: Self-hosted only — no official managed hosting plan
Flat-file storage can be slower than a database for very large wikis
No official native mobile app

faqs.question: Is DokuWiki free?
faqs.answer: Yes, DokuWiki is free and open source, licensed under GPL-2.0.

faqs.question: Does DokuWiki need a database?
faqs.answer: No — DokuWiki stores pages, revisions, and metadata as plain text files instead of using a database.

faqs.question: Who maintains DokuWiki?
faqs.answer: DokuWiki is maintained by creator Andreas Gohr and the DokuWiki community, with copyright dating to 2004.

faqs.question: Where can I get DokuWiki support or plugins?
faqs.answer: Documentation, installation guides, and a plugin directory are available at dokuwiki.org.

use_cases.title: Documentation without database overhead
use_cases.description: Teams that want to avoid running a database server use DokuWiki's flat-file storage.

use_cases.title: Personal or project wikis
use_cases.description: Individuals and open-source projects self-host DokuWiki for lightweight note and documentation storage.

use_cases.title: Corporate intranets
use_cases.description: Organizations use DokuWiki's access control lists to manage internal documentation with restricted permissions.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is DokuWiki?
paragraphs: DokuWiki is a free, open-source wiki engine, first released in 2004 by Andreas Gohr, that stores all pages, revisions, and metadata as plain text files instead of requiring a database.
It's released under the GPL-2.0 license, runs on PHP, and is extended through a large ecosystem of community plugins and templates.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who DokuWiki is for
paragraphs: DokuWiki suits teams and individuals who want a lightweight, self-hosted wiki without the overhead of setting up and maintaining a database server.
Its access control lists and low system requirements also make it a fit for smaller organizations or personal projects running on modest hosting.

enrichment.short_description: XWiki is an open-source enterprise wiki platform, free to self-host as Community edition, with paid XWiki Cloud plans for teams.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: XWiki: open-source (LGPL-2.1) enterprise wiki platform, free to self-host as Community edition, with paid Cloud plans for teams and enterprises.

features.title: Structured wiki pages
features.description: Organize knowledge using structured wiki pages built on the XWiki Platform.

features.title: Self-hosted Community edition
features.description: Free, open-source edition (LGPL-2.1) that can be self-hosted for up to 25 users.

features.title: XWiki Cloud hosting
features.description: Cloud plans (Starter, Basic, Business, Enterprise) offer hosted deployment without managing servers.

features.title: On-premise deployment
features.description: Basic, Business, and Enterprise plans support on-premise as well as cloud deployment.

features.title: Identity and SSO integrations
features.description: Higher-tier plans support multiple identity providers and SCIM provisioning.

features.title: Extensible applications
features.description: The platform can be extended into custom collaborative applications beyond a traditional wiki.

pros: Free, open-source Community edition (LGPL-2.1)
Choice of self-hosted or cloud (XWiki Cloud) deployment
Free XWiki Cloud Starter hosting offered to open-source projects
Scales from small teams (Community, 25 users) to enterprise (unlimited users)
Discounts available for NGOs and academic institutions
cons: Cloud plan pricing (Starter through Enterprise) is not publicly listed and requires contacting sales
Community edition is capped at 25 users
Advanced identity/SCIM features are limited to higher-priced tiers

faqs.question: Is XWiki free?
faqs.answer: The Community edition is free and open source (LGPL-2.1) and can be self-hosted for up to 25 users.

faqs.question: Does XWiki offer cloud hosting?
faqs.answer: Yes, XWiki Cloud offers Starter, Basic, Business, and Enterprise plans, billed yearly or on 3-year terms.

faqs.question: Is XWiki Cloud pricing public?
faqs.answer: No — pricing for the Starter, Basic, Business, and Enterprise cloud plans is not publicly listed and requires contacting XWiki's sales team.

faqs.question: Does XWiki offer discounts?
faqs.answer: Yes, XWiki offers free Starter cloud hosting for open-source projects and discounts for NGOs and academic institutions.

use_cases.title: Enterprise knowledge management
use_cases.description: Larger organizations use XWiki's Business or Enterprise cloud/on-premise plans as an alternative to Confluence or SharePoint.
use_cases.audience: Enterprise IT and knowledge management teams

use_cases.title: Self-hosted team wikis
use_cases.description: Smaller teams self-host the free Community edition for internal documentation.

use_cases.title: Custom collaborative applications
use_cases.description: Developers build structured applications on top of the XWiki Platform beyond standard wiki pages.

features: Custom domain, SCIM, dedicated account manager

long_form_blocks.heading: What is XWiki?
paragraphs: XWiki is an open-source wiki platform, released under the LGPL-2.1 license, that offers structured pages and can be extended into custom collaborative applications.

The free, self-hostable Community edition supports up to 25 users, while XWiki SAS (the commercial entity behind xwiki.com) offers paid XWiki Cloud plans — Starter, Basic, Business, and Enterprise — with cloud or on-premise deployment for larger teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who XWiki is for
paragraphs: XWiki fits small teams and open-source projects that just need a free, self-hosted structured wiki, as well as larger organizations wanting a hosted or on-premise alternative to Confluence or SharePoint with SSO and identity provisioning.
Its tiered Cloud plans (up to 1,000, 10,000, or unlimited users) let organizations scale without managing their own infrastructure.

enrichment.short_description: MediaWiki is the free, open-source wiki software that powers Wikipedia and is used by thousands of other websites.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: MediaWiki: the free, open-source wiki software behind Wikipedia, maintained by a community, built for multilingual, extensible collaboration.

features.title: Powers Wikipedia
features.description: The same software that runs Wikipedia and other Wikimedia projects.

features.title: Multilingual
features.description: Built to support content and interfaces in many languages.

features.title: Extensible
features.description: Customizable through a large ecosystem of extensions.

features.title: Free and open source
features.description: Released as free, open-source software available to download and self-host.

features.title: Community-maintained
features.description: Developed and maintained by a community of volunteer contributors.

features.title: Support desk and bug tracking
features.description: Users can get help via a support desk and report issues through established bug-tracking channels.

pros: Free and open source, proven at Wikipedia scale
Highly extensible via a large library of extensions
Multilingual support out of the box
Backed by an active, long-running open-source community
Widely documented with installation, configuration, and admin guides
cons: Self-hosted setup and server maintenance required unless using a commercial host
No official managed hosting from the MediaWiki project itself
Can require technical expertise to configure and extend

faqs.question: Is MediaWiki free?
faqs.answer: Yes, MediaWiki is free, open-source software.

faqs.question: What powers Wikipedia?
faqs.answer: Wikipedia runs on MediaWiki.

faqs.question: Can I get MediaWiki hosting?
faqs.answer: The project itself is free to download and self-host; users without their own servers can use third-party commercial hosting services.

faqs.question: Is professional support available?
faqs.answer: Yes, professional development and consulting services are available from third parties, alongside community support channels.

faqs.question: What was the latest release at the time of writing?
faqs.answer: MediaWiki 1.46.0, per the project's release announcements.

use_cases.title: Public encyclopedias and reference wikis
use_cases.description: Organizations run large-scale public knowledge bases the same way Wikipedia does.

use_cases.title: Internal corporate wikis
use_cases.description: Companies self-host MediaWiki for internal documentation and knowledge sharing.

use_cases.title: Extensible community platforms
use_cases.description: Developers extend MediaWiki with custom extensions for specialized collaborative sites.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is MediaWiki?
paragraphs: MediaWiki is the free, open-source wiki software that powers Wikipedia and is used by tens of thousands of other websites to collect and organize knowledge.

It's maintained by a community of volunteer contributors, is highly extensible, and supports multilingual content, with the software free of charge and optional commercial hosting or development services available from third parties.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who MediaWiki is for
paragraphs: MediaWiki fits organizations that need a proven, large-scale wiki platform — from public encyclopedic projects to internal corporate knowledge bases — and are comfortable self-hosting or contracting a third party to do so.
Its extension ecosystem also makes it a fit for developers who want to customize wiki functionality beyond the defaults.

features.title: Single-file wiki
features.description: Runs as a complete, self-contained wiki inside a single HTML file that opens in any browser.

features.title: Non-linear notes
features.description: Organizes notes as interlinked 'tiddlers' rather than a fixed linear structure.

features.title: Node.js server mode
features.description: Can also run as a Node.js application for a client/server setup.

features.title: Hackable interface
features.description: The entire user interface is implemented in editable WikiText, making it customizable.

features.title: Multiple hosting options
features.description: Can be self-hosted as a file, run via Node.js/AWS Lambda, or hosted via the independent Tiddlyhost service.

pros: Free and open source (BSD-3-Clause license)
No server or database required — runs as a single portable HTML file
Highly customizable, hackable interface
Can also run as a Node.js app for more advanced setups
Long-running project maintained since 2004 with an active community
cons: Saving changes from the browser's File > Save menu does not work, which can confuse new users
Single-file/local model means no built-in real-time multi-user collaboration
Advanced customization requires learning its WikiText scripting system

faqs.question: Is TiddlyWiki free?
faqs.answer: Yes, TiddlyWiki is free and open source, released under the BSD-3-Clause license.

faqs.question: Do I need to install anything to use TiddlyWiki?
faqs.answer: No — it runs as a single HTML file that can be opened directly in a browser, though it can also run via Node.js.

faqs.question: Can I save changes using the browser's File > Save menu?
faqs.answer: No, the project explicitly warns that using the browser's File/Save menu doesn't work; other saving methods are required depending on OS and browser.

faqs.question: Is there a hosted option?
faqs.answer: Yes, Tiddlyhost is an independently run community service offering free hosted TiddlyWiki accounts.

faqs.question: Who created TiddlyWiki?
faqs.answer: TiddlyWiki was created by Jeremy Ruston, first released in 2004; its copyright is now held in trust by the UnaMesa Association.

use_cases.title: Personal knowledge management
use_cases.description: Individuals use TiddlyWiki's non-linear tiddler system to capture and interlink personal notes.

use_cases.title: Portable offline notebooks
use_cases.description: Users who want a notebook that works without installing software carry TiddlyWiki as a single HTML file.

use_cases.title: Custom wiki applications
use_cases.description: Developers use TiddlyWiki's hackable WikiText interface and Node.js mode to build customized tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is TiddlyWiki?
paragraphs: TiddlyWiki is a free, open-source personal notebook, first released in 2004 by creator Jeremy Ruston, that runs as a complete interactive wiki packed into a single HTML file.
It's released under the BSD-3-Clause license, can also run as a Node.js application for client/server setups, and its entire interface is built in editable WikiText, making it deeply customizable.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who TiddlyWiki is for
paragraphs: TiddlyWiki suits individuals who want a portable, non-linear personal notebook that doesn't depend on any company's servers or software installation.
Its hackable WikiText interface and Node.js mode also make it a fit for developers who want to build customized note-taking or documentation tools on top of it.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Logseq?
paragraphs: Logseq is a free, open-source (AGPL-3.0) knowledge base app that stores notes locally in Markdown or Org-mode files and links them into a knowledge graph.

It includes PDF annotation, task management, and a plugin/theme ecosystem, with desktop apps for macOS, Linux, and Windows, iOS and Android mobile apps, a web version, and an in-development Real Time Collaboration feature for multi-device sync.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Logseq is for
paragraphs: Logseq fits individuals who want a privacy-first, local-first note-taking tool and prefer plain-text Markdown or Org-mode files over a proprietary format.
Its plugin ecosystem and cross-platform apps also make it a fit for researchers and knowledge workers who want to extend or sync their notes across desktop and mobile.

Discounts for students, faculty, and contributors
No centralized server required for core use

faqs.question: Is Anytype free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Anytype has a free membership tier with 100 MB of remote storage, 10 shared spaces, and unlimited private spaces.

faqs.question: Does Anytype require the internet?
faqs.answer: No, it's offline-first — data is stored locally, with optional peer-to-peer sync across devices.

faqs.question: Is Anytype open source?
faqs.answer: Anytype publishes its source code under the Any Source Available License 1.0, and is maintained by Any, a Swiss association.

faqs.question: What platforms does Anytype support?
faqs.answer: Desktop apps for macOS, Windows, and Linux, mobile apps for iOS and Android, and a web clipper for Firefox and Chromium browsers.

faqs.question: Are there paid plans?
faqs.answer: Yes — Plus, Pro, and Ultra membership tiers offer more remote storage (1 GB, 10 GB, and 100 GB respectively) beyond the free tier.

use_cases.title: Custom structured databases

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Anytype?
paragraphs: Anytype is an offline-first personal knowledge base app that stores data locally and encrypts it end-to-end, syncing across devices peer-to-peer instead of relying on a central company server.
It's built around composable blocks — text, databases, kanban boards, calendars, and custom object Types — and is maintained by Any, a Swiss association, under the Any Source Available License 1.0.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Anytype is for
paragraphs: Anytype fits individuals and teams who want a privacy-first alternative to cloud note-taking apps, with data staying on-device unless they opt into sync.
Its free tier covers personal use with limited remote storage, while paid Plus, Pro, and Ultra memberships add more remote storage for those syncing across multiple devices or sharing spaces.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Affine is for

long_form_blocks.heading: What is AppFlowy?
paragraphs: AppFlowy is an open-source workspace application that combines documents, wikis, and project databases with a built-in AI assistant. It is positioned as a customizable, data-ownership-focused alternative to closed workspace tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who AppFlowy is for

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Milanote?
paragraphs: Milanote is a visual organization tool for creative projects that lets users combine notes, images, video, and sketches on flexible boards. It includes industry-specific templates for filmmaking, writing, design, photography, and marketing.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Plane?
paragraphs: Plane is an AI-native project management platform combining task tracking, documentation, and AI-powered workflows in one workspace. It offers Board, Spreadsheet, List, and Gantt views, time-boxed cycles, a built-in wiki, and real-time dashboards.

Plane is available as a free cloud tier for up to 12 users, paid Pro and Business cloud tiers, a custom-quoted Enterprise Grid plan, and self-hosted commercial or air-gapped editions with migration tools from Jira, Linear, ClickUp, Asana, and Monday.com.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is OpenProject?
paragraphs: OpenProject is an open-source project management platform offering Gantt charts, agile Scrum and Kanban boards, a Team Planner, time tracking, and cost reporting. It is licensed under GPL v3 and can be self-hosted or used via Enterprise Cloud.

Paid tiers (Basic, Professional, Premium, Corporate) add enterprise features and support levels, with a 14-day free trial available for cloud and on-premises versions. It integrates with tools including Nextcloud, GitHub, GitLab, and OneDrive/SharePoint.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who OpenProject is for
paragraphs: OpenProject suits organizations that want an open-source, self-hostable project management tool, as well as enterprises that need professional support and Enterprise Cloud hosting.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Leantime?
paragraphs: Leantime is an open-source project management platform positioned as "project management for the non-project manager," designed specifically for neurodivergent users such as people with ADHD, dyslexia, and autism.

It offers a personal My Work Dashboard, Kanban/Table/List views, Gantt charts, whiteboards, timesheets, and AI-powered task prioritization. The Community Edition is free and open source (AGPLv3) for self-hosting, while a hosted cloud Pro plan starts at $8/user/month billed annually, with a 14-day free trial.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Leantime is for
paragraphs: Leantime is aimed at individuals and teams, including neurodivergent professionals, who want a simplified, goal-based project management workflow, whether self-hosted for free or via the paid cloud plan.
